sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE] Now, we'll continue a little bit later.
I don't know if it was in-- if it was in July or it was in August.
Naturally, I didn't look at the time on the calendar.
I know and I walked the street, and I saw a German with a long leather coat, even though it was July.
It was a warm day.
And he had a Hakenkreuz-- it means  a swastika-- on his arm ban, with a high hat.
And written down on the hat-- because I had good eyesight then, and I took a look.
I see-- when I saw him, I saw that it  was written on, [? 'Der Tod.' ?] And he had with them a dog, a German dog, like a-- like a-- like a wolf, a big dog.

And I saw an old Jewish man walking across the street, near-- not on the sidewalk, but near the sidewalk, on the-- where the [INAUDIBLE] were running, because a Jew is not supposed to walk on the sidewalk.
Walking on the sidewalk is the penalty of death.
Getting water by the river is penalty of death.
And I'll explain to you what water of the river what has happened.
First, I'll start with [INAUDIBLE] this SS man.
I don't know his name.
He is anonymous.
But I saw his actions.
All of us saw and heard the German holler to the dog-- Jude! 'Jude' in German means Jew.
And he pointed to this man walking across the street on the side-- near the sidewalk.
And the dog ran after him, turned around, jumped up his hind legs on-- across his throat-- dropped him.
All I hear is the man screamed, oy!
And I saw two big streams of blood, maybe three or four feet high, coming out of his throat.
And the dog killed him.
And he called him back.
I was nearly-- I was out of my mind.
I started-- I tried to start running.
I was shocked.
I couldn't ran.
I couldn't do-- I didn't know what to do.
But I saw the way he called the dog back, and he petted the dog.
He bent down, something.
I don't if he kissed him or something.
And then he took something out of his pocket to give to the dog.

The [INAUDIBLE] one of our prophecies, and I forgot their names, that they said there will come a day that the men would not have pity at the suckling-- a baby suckling milk with his mother-- or an old man with a gray beard.
They'll kill-- kiss the calf and kiss the dog.
This prophecy was what come-- come true.
I start running any time, even in America.
When I pass by the car door and I see a man come with a big dog, and I get scared.
My heart starts pumping to me, because it's with me.
I will see the picture of one [INAUDIBLE] till the day I die.
The way a dog is capable of saving a man, a German dog, if he's taught.
And he's capable of killing a  man in seconds, in minutes.
He just ripped his throat apart.
And it was unbelievable.
I never believed a dog could do such things.
And then I'll tell you an incident maybe it happened after this incident.
There's another incident where the Germans gave an order the Jewish people cannot go to the river because the river passed the ghetto.
One part of the ghetto was a river.
As a matter of fact, the ghetto was partly surrounded by the river.
So the Jews would not be able to run away, because by us, they didn't put a big-- what do you call, only fence they put up.
They didn't put any stone what do you mean-- construction.
They shouldn't be able to get out.
So they wanted to starve the people out.
So they decided that a Jew cannot go to the river, because a little bit-- two miles, three miles away, was a German plaza.
And there were the Germans going to bathe.
And they didn't want the dreckige Jude-- dreckige means the dirty Jews-- to contaminate the water for them because they are going-- they have to go swimming in the river.
So they gave the order under penalty of death, nobody could get to the river to fetch water.
Early in the morning, apparently, the woman didn't have enough-- didn't-- didn't get the water the day before, because they shut the water off.
And she went early in the morning before this-- it became light.
And she got fetched a pail of water.
And a German caught her, and he dragged to be shot.
And a little girl went with her-- the little girl from four years, holding on to her dress.
And the Germans took her.
And she never came back home.
They killed her for it.
For fetching a little water.
Why?
They wanted to put fear in the people's heart.
Because the Germans were afraid that maybe, maybe the Jews taking a little water would contaminate the water where they were swimming.
This was horrible.
It's un-- it's-- it's-- the people starved to death in-- in-- in the city of my hometown where I lived.
By the fall of this year, by-- by December.
As a matter of fact, this incident, this chasing people in the ghetto was that my grandmother had a daughter, Chava.
She was suffering with polio.
She couldn't walk.
So she put her on a little sled, and she tried to pull her.
And the sled was not enough-- snow enough.
The ground was mixed snow and mud.
So she kept on pulling.
She was very, very hard to pull.
She couldn't pull fast enough.
A German came off, took his revolver, shot my-- my Aunt Chava.
And while they shot her, she fell off of the-- of the sled.
And he-- and he pushed her in with the foot into the-- into the-- the street.
In Europe, have on both sides of the street have, like, little-- little-- little dips in the ground so the water should run off.
So he pushed her in there.
It was full of mud-- water and mud.
And he said to my grandmother, now you can go faster.
Now you can pull.
And my grandmother could imagine how she felt.
Anybody who had a sick person couldn't go fast or they killed.
They killed several hundred people on the way going-- pushing them into the ghetto.
The ghetto area-- had any Jews lived there before?
Yeah.
Yeah.
The ghetto, yeah, Jews lived before.
But they pushed all the people from the-- from the ghetto into this particular area where it crowded up so much that, for example, my mother with her-- with her five children.
I was-- my father smuggled me out there before.
That's what I want to tell.

There was a man-- his name was Schlomo Berger, and he-- my father begged him he said smuggle me out, from there-- from-- because while the Germans [INAUDIBLE] people in the ghetto, they didn't count how many people are there, you know, because there, everybody was pushed into the ghetto.
Then later on, if you are not registered, you don't get food.
If you don't get food, you die.
There's no place to get food.
It's all surrounded.
You can't go out.
You have to go out with a special [INAUDIBLE].
To go to work, you have to have something.
So they lived in a little room, I would say four by-- by eight.
My father built a little wooden-- wooden little benches on the [INAUDIBLE] where the kids used to sleep one, one, one on top of the other in order to be able to sleep through.
And it was very horrible.
The people who lived on the spot had already better.
They could get the best room of the-- of the apartment.
But other people crowded in, and they crowded them in one area.
And then they brought in people also from the outlying areas, not just from the--
Yeah, they brought in people from outlying areas.
As    a matter of fact, I-- from all the outlying villages, they brought down people.
And they-- of course the ghetto became overfilled more and more.
So they found it two ways.
Either people just starving to death, they just die out.
And then they have control that they keep the people locked up in one-- one place.
My father, my brother, and some other people had a [INAUDIBLE], a working [INAUDIBLE] to go and work on the-- on the highway, going from Luck to Rocyzscze-- 19 kilometers that town, Rocyzscze.
I happened to be working by a Czech-- a Czech farmer that my father smuggled me out of there to take me out to the farmer.
And he gave me over to a farmer just for food, he should keep me, and I should be able to work for the farmer.
Needless to say, the hardest jobs I did for the farmer.
I took care of the cows and the horses and I plowed.
I was a young kid, but I was very strong.
But I had food, at least, by the farmer.
In the fall when the ghetto started.
My father wouldn't let me go stay in the ghetto.
He smuggled me out.
And he sent-- he sent me away to the farm.
No, Czechoslovakia is a country.
But we were in a-- in a village.
I was in a village seven kilometers from my hometown named [INAUDIBLE].

And they were strict Czech people-- Czech people from Slovakia. 250 Years, or maybe 300 years, the tsarina of Russia decided to teach her peasants how to operate, how to work on the land, and how to make the land productive.
She imported Czechs and Germans from Germany and from Czechs.
They should teach the population how to be good farmers, which was-- they were very excellent.
They were already European development-- the Western development.
So they-- they made colonies.
Colonies means like a separate colony, separate ethnic people in a separate town-- in a separate colony.
A colony is like a big village.
And this Czech took me in to work for him because he had a lot of land, and he needed to have working people.
And nobody-- he couldn't get anybody.
So he was glad to help me, and I was glad to work there for nothing.

So when my father passed by with my brother on the-- on the road to go to work, he passed by, I was standing there already near the gutter, waiting and tried to give him some-- little bit of barley, maybe a potato, maybe a piece of bread.
So in one way, my father could smuggle it in.
My father had a pair of big boots summertime.
How could you-- even wintertime.
How could you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the big boots, he used to carry a pair of boots on his-- on his shoulders.
And he could carry a pair of shoes-- ripped shoes on his-- on his feet.
So he put-- by us, he couldn't afford any socks.
So we used to use rags to wrap around the feet.
So my father used to put a piece of potato, a piece of bread, in the boot, and then put the dirty rags on the top so the Germans figure that he carries working clothes back to the ghetto.
Meanwhile, he smuggled them.
Because one kid was found carrying a cup of potatoes.
He was shot for it.
The Germans wanted to starve the people, so they made strict-- if anybody is caught carrying some food or bread, or something, or apple, or pear, or whatever, you can-- or anything.
I could-- needless to say, on the road, I gave them something to eat, because I had plenty to eat because the farmer was very rich.
But my father smuggled in very little bit-- whatever he can-- in order to feed the other children in the ghetto.
No.
No, since I worked, I took off the star.
Of course, this was the penalty of death if they catch me.
But I relied on the Czech.
He told me that all the Czechs are a family.
They are all intermarried.
They are all the same people.
There are no Ukrainians coming there.
Nobody would know that I am Jewish, especially will give me a garb dressed up in clothes like a farmer.
And I am going to work in a field where nobody comes near.
If I stay with the horses and plow, nobody goes there.
Everybody is busy.
And I keep out of the way.
I keep a low profile.
I started there in fall in 1941, 12th of December.
And I was there in the same farm till the 18th of August, 1942, when the Germans started to systematically taking the people out and kill them.
The rest of the family, they were starving from hunger.
They were like skeletons.
But whatever my father could bring in and smuggle in from the street, because summertime, he worked on-- on the street, to fix-- on the highway to fix the holes in the-- the pavement.
Wintertime, he worked to shovel the snow.
And was fortunate that this year, 1941, was a very, very, very bad winter.
And they had to take the people from the ghetto to shovel the snow for the Germans.
They should be able to go with their machines and plugs.
So they took more and more, every day, the people out.
And my father had to pass by the place where I was on the farm.
And I knew already when he was coming and when he's going.
And he had a Polish supervisor, and the Polish supervisor liked my father because he was-- my father was a friend of his.
When he used to come to our house, he used to give him beer to drink.
My father worked in a brewery.
So he overlooked.
And my father ran down, and I had already something for him to eat from my brother.
And then he smuggled in something to the ghetto.
But the people, they kept on dying from dysentery or from sickness in the ghetto.
People kept starving.
But somehow, somewhere, my-- my-- my family, my mother, survived.
Meanwhile, my brother-in-law sneaked out of the same ghetto of Luck, and he was taken in by a Polish family because he was a tailor.
His name is Kissel Aivainer, which I spell-- spelled the name Aivainer the way I said before.
And he smuggled his wife out, because she was very blonde.
My family, with some children blonde, like my father.
Some children were dark like me.
I looked like my mother.
My mother was dark.
So she could easily pass as a Gentile woman, because she spoke Polish perfectly.
And the two children which she took with her and settled down near-- in a village near a wood where, hopefully, she could have survived, because she had a big chance to survive.
If she was living alone in the village and not surrounded with people, she could have survived.
But she decided she wanted to see her mother in the ghetto.
She wanted to see her mother in the ghetto.
She started crying for her mother in the ghetto.
My brother-in-law couldn't stop her, and they-- she said, the farmer says, you know, I have to go to the city Thursday to do some work for the Germans.
I'll take you along.
So she was given milk to the little baby-- the baby was only nine months old.
She took the baby along.
The bigger girl, the three-year-old girl, went after her and started screaming.
So the farmer, oh, go, take the [INAUDIBLE] little girl too.
My sister put on her kerchief, and she looked like a-- like a farmer's wife.
And she went to the ghetto.
She jumped over the two children.
She went to see my mother.
At 5:00, the farmer-- wonderful gentleman, a Pollack, wanted to-- a Polish fellow-- he wanted to pick my-- my sister up, because he was promising he was going to take her back.
They wouldn't let him in.
The Germans and Ukrainians, the army of German soldiers, army of Ukrainians, surrounded the whole area, and they wouldn't let nobody in.
The next day, the selections started.
And the thing that I'm going to say about the ghetto, what has happened, is only because I read from it what people-- what survivors told, because I was not in the ghetto at this time.
I was out by the farmer.
And the way they started systematically driving the Jew-- the people into the-- seven kilometers away in a different direction.
Not direction that I was working, but in a north direction of the city towards Rovno.
There the name of the town-- little village is [INAUDIBLE], where the big grave site they dug.
And the irony of this that the night before, Jewish workers, jewish laborers had to dig the graves.
And they dig the graves.
They were so bold, the Germans, to-- the Ukrainians watched them, and the Ukrainians made fun of them.
The graves, you dig good.
Dig very good.
These are the graves for your wives and children.
And the Germans were so, so adamant to take-- to see if anybody has maybe have a five golden piece in pocket.
Maybe somebody put it-- a ring, like, my mother wouldn't give part of her ring.
She would not give it.
She would say, I will keep it.
And under penalty of death, the Germans, they, whenever they find gold, they will kill.
And they made the women and the young girls, [INAUDIBLE] to undress.
The screaming, the hollering was terrible.
Dogs and with whips they used to beat the people as you go around.
They walked down into the-- into the grave.
The grave was 18 by-- by about 40 feet long.
And they made the people lay out like herrings.
And the Germans were sitting at the top of the graveside with the-- with the arm-- arms like [INAUDIBLE] like this, with the machine guns over [? their lap. ?] And they kept on shooting the people.
Then a man went in-- inside the grave with big, long boots, and he kept on shooting whatever person was still moving.
Then they took another group, another group.
They kept on taking.
This was going on for-- for several days and nights.
At nighttime, when I was with the farmer, we saw the ghetto burning.
The farmer came into the-- to the-- to the barn where I was sleeping.
And he says, kluku.
Kluku means a farmer boy.
Boy, run for your life.
I can't keep you any longer.
I'm afraid, because the Germans gave an order, if anybody would give a-- give a Jew shelter, or give him a piece of bread to eat, they will kill the  whole family.
And I'm afraid for my family.
I was very good to you.
I kept you till now.
And I said-- And I took off, and I ran into the woods.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Summer of 1942.
And what happened is my father went, this same night when he went home, from the road, my-- my father's sister, Susya, sent her two kids.

And my two brothers-- the little brother David, and Abraham, and-- and-- and Maya-- they were all blonde kids-- sent them out-- out to warn their father, which was my Uncle Berl Nayman, they should go back, because a lot of people came.
But the Jew-- whenever any Jew came the same night back, they let them in.
But they wouldn't let nobody out.
So the little girl-- [INAUDIBLE], the taller girl-- says to her father, father, mother said we shouldn't go, because the Germans and the Ukrainians surrounded the ghetto.
This may be for real.
They may scare us, but this is for real, the things.
So his father-- their-- the girl's father told my father.
My father was good natured.
He couldn't hold it to himself.
He told other people.
So many of the other people did not want to believe that the Germans are capable of doing that.
How is it possible?
No, I'll go home.
My wife and children are home.
I'll go home.
But my father with my uncle, with the children, went back, went to the colony with me.
And we met-- met in the nighttime in the back of the barns.
The Czech-- I didn't work for him anymore.
And we made-- formed a group.
And we were all trying to survive in the back of the barns, because in the back of the barns are big fields.
The woods are not far away.
And meanwhile, in the field, there was a lot of food to eat because he has tomatoes, he has cucumbers-- there's all kind of field.
And then this-- this-- this story is a story of [INAUDIBLE].
The same Czechs are wonderful people.
But they decided that we as people, surviving-- trying to survive-- are somehow getting into the gardens.
And some of them, real-- not farmers like myself, did damage to the tomatoes.
They pulled the-- the-- the potatoes.
And they took all the potatoes with the-- out.
And even the row, they didn't-- they didn't put back everything.
They even-- they did things that the farmers didn't like.
Of course, we were fighting for our lives-- for our survival.

So lo and behold, the same people I worked for-- the same people I spoke Czech with very well-- they decided to catch us and to threaten us, or beat us up, or give us over to the Germans because there is a rumor the Germans said they would  only kill two days, three days.
From now on, they're not going to kill anymore.
This was all deceit.
The Germans used deceit.
[INAUDIBLE] they used the biggest psychiatrists to deceive our people.
So all of a sudden, I go to look for food.
I see kids, young kids, ran after me.
And they caught me.
They caught more people.
They took us to the starosta.
The starosta means the mayor of the village.
And I was the only single person who understands Czechs.
It's Slavic, but different than Polish, different than Russian.
And they keep on saying, the majority said, no let's beat them up.
And let's tell them, listen, get the hell out of here.
We don't want you to damage our gardens.
And some people who were not careful, they ripped the tomatoes.
They did everything-- damage to the gardens.
So on all of a sudden I see one says, listen, we heard the Germans gave an order that any Jews alive could go back to ghetto and nothing-- no harm  will happen to them.
We'll call up the Germans.
What do we have to barter with them.
We'll ring the bell-- the telephone-- the telephone, you ring like this, old telephone-- and we'll go to call the Germans.
And he's going to the telephone to call the Germans.
It takes 10 minutes, seven kilometers, to come to grab us.
And we were about six or eight people they caught.
My father and brother they didn't catch.
So I stood up and said to them in Czech.
I said, wait a minute.
You give us over to the Germans.
My father and brother is alive.
And if you give me over to the Germans to give my friends-- my pa-- my-- my friends over to the Germans, the Germans are all deceiving us.
They're going to kill us.
The whole colony will go up in smoke.
When I said smoke, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a deadly silence became in this big town-- big-- big-- big hall, big room, for the mayor's room.
Fire?
A farmer's afraid for fire.
He's sleeping in this beautiful house, but in the back, he has a barn full of straw.
And he has big straw, haystacks, and big barns with straw.
And they were came talking to me.
Listen, you know, we took care of you.
You worked for us.
Please, tell your compatriots-- I still remember the word compatriots.
I'll never forget it.
Compatriots mean friends.
And I didn't know the compatriots would be dead.
Now I know.
Tell your compatriots if they go in the garden, let them take only the-- the ripe potatoes-- tomatoes.
Or let them take the ripe food.
Let them not damage-- let them not damage this.
And besides, if you have to go, let them cover up, like the Bible said, they teach me the Bible.
Of course, a lot of people didn't care.
But I tell-- I'll tell my friends, we will not touch you.
We will not bother you.
But be careful, because if the Germans find out that you are here, they will make a [NON-ENGLISH].
A [NON-ENGLISH] means surrounding  the village.
And they'll catch you all, so you'd better be careful.
To me they told me.
And they let us go.
Nobody beat us.
Nobody did any harm to us.
I saved the day.
The other guy said to me, what did you say?
How come [INAUDIBLE] they became so nice to us?
I say, because they wanted-- I threatened them with fire.
And this was the truth.
When we were back out, my father and the other group decided, now's not the time to sit because the Germans will find out there are Jews.
Maybe some vicious person will tell the Germans.
They'll surround the area with Ukrainians.
They'll pick everybody up.
Let's go and look for Partisans.
And here, the saga of my life starts.

In my family, was my-- myself, my dad, my older brother, Eliezer, my younger sister, Maya, my younger brother, Abraham, and my youngest boy, a five-year-old child, who didn't know what was going on was there-- five years old.
And other people together-- we were about 26 or 28 people.
Naturally, 28 people could do a lot of damage in a village from the gardens.
You have to go slow.
They didn't know the gardens like we do.
So we decided to go to the woods.
Taken, [INTERPOSING VOICES] and then the ghetto's burned-- every night the ghetto iis burning because a lot of Jews did not want to be taken like this.
They made a fire.
They fought the Germans and Ukrainians.
They killed a couple of Ukrainians and a couple of Germans, because they knew this was the end.
So they burned the ghetto.
They figured, [NON-ENGLISH]-- we should die together with them.
And they did-- they didn't want to leave anything over for them.
And every night, we saw the flames and the big-- the sky is red from the site of the town where the ghetto is.
Because seven kilometers, you could see it.
In the dark of the night, you could see the city burning.
But this was the ghetto burning.
So now, there is a saga of another cap-- another-- another chapter of our lives.
The 28 people go into the woods.
28 People-- no, no, no.
From the 28 people, only 18 decided to go to the woods.

Between the people that decided to go to the woods was me, my-- I started my father, my Uncle Berl Nayman-- which his children saved my father and them to go back-- and my brother, and other people that I forgot.
There was-- there was a-- a friend of mine wanted me together in school with his brother.
The rest of the people I forgot.
Needless to say, the youngest of the whole group-- I was the youngest.
So this is a-- this is a good question that you ask, because I am about to say to you.
So my father says to me, Milton, Eliezer-- we were the oldest-- come.
Let's find a-- a hiding place for the children.
And we will find somebody in the woods.
We will come back and get them.
Meanwhile, we will be able-- because you cannot schlep a five-year-old kid in the darkness at night.
And we don't know what we'll find in the way.
He will hold us back.
We'll go ourselves, and we'll come back.
We started working feverishly in a big haystack.
And we dug a hole in the haystack.
And I made a hole place-- a place for them, for my older-- for my older-- younger sister, Maya, for Abraham and David, and three children of my uncle.
So together, there are six children together-- my two brothers and sister and my-- my uncle's two children and a little boy.
The boy is already seven years old.
So there was three and three-- six children.
And we gave them instructions-- don't go out in the daytime.
Go at night, and please, hide yourself.
Be careful in the gardens.
Take only tomatoes what you want.
Take apples from the trees.
Take pears, whatever, because it was still-- still summertime.
August was summertime.
And we went to the woods.
We went across a river.
I'm not going to make a big story short.
[INAUDIBLE] story short went across the river.
We went to the woods-- Kivertsi.
Kivertsi is 17 kilometers, really big woods that go deep into Russia, from my hometown-- Kivertsi.
It's the name on the atlas.
I saw it's still there.
We went there.
We came to the woods, and we were in the woods.
We're looking.
All of sudden, my uncle-- what's the name?
Berl says he wants to go in the village.
Maybe he has a couple of things with him in his-- with him.
He has a couple of stocking socks.
Maybe he'll go and give it to the farmer.
The farmer will give him bread.
He never came back.
And next day was Yom Kippur.
Yom Kippur was the day before-- the 24th was the day when my father was caught.
And we didn't want to move from the place because some boy and girl was looking around for nuts.
A gentile-- a Ukrainian-- they partly discovered our place.
But they didn't discover our-- our-- what do you-- our cave.
We had a cave made out in the woods, you know, to hide.
And needless to say, a day after Yom Kippur, I went with my father to look for wood in order to burn-- make a fire and make potatoes on the way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were caught.
We were caught by the-- by the Ukrainians.
I didn't see Germans.
One Ukrainian was with a-- with a bicycle.
One Ukrainian had a gun on his shoulder.
Another Ukrainian had a gun in his hand.
And he caught me first.
He said, [NON-ENGLISH].
He started patting my pockets.
In the pockets, I had some nuts, figuring I would go to see my-- my-- my brother and sister.
I'll bring him some-- some from the woods.
And he started checking me out if I have any-- [INAUDIBLE] I was a young kid.
All of a sudden, I see my father comes over.
My father was far away, and he could have escaped.
We were looking for wood.
And he took us away from the cave where we was hidden, and he took us away in the other direction.
And has, [NON-ENGLISH].
Lift up the hand, go.
And my father was going in the back of me.
Just the two of me.
I didn't know what happened to the group.
I did not know yet.
But this is what happened.
And they got the two of us.
And the Ukrainian was marching you-- you and your father away.
Me and my father.
And I'll tell you the feeling, when a man is marched to be-- to be shot.
